moveAnnotations.annotation: The Benko Gambit is an important variation of the Benko Gambit. Understanding this line will strengthen your repertoire. Let's walk through the key ideas.
Black replies with Nf6. This is the defining move of the Benko Gambit — it shapes the pawn structure and determines the resulting plans.
c4 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
c5 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
d5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
b5 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
cxb5 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — White reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
a6 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
bxa6 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — White reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
Bxa6 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on a6 eyes important squares.
Nc3 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on c3 eyes important squares.
d6 increases Black's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position.
e4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
Bxf1 captures the bishop. This exchange changes the balance — Black reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
Kxf1 captures the bishop. This exchange changes the balance — White reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
g6 pushes on the kingside. This pawn advance gains space and can support a future attack toward the enemy king.
g3 pushes on the kingside. This pawn advance gains space and can support a future attack toward the enemy king.
Bg7 — Black fianchettoes the bishop, placing it on the long diagonal where it controls the center from a distance. This is a key positional idea.
Kg2 improves the king's placement. The king was less effective on f1 and moves to g2 where it serves the plan better.
Black castles kingside to tuck the king away safely and activate the rook. Connecting the rooks is a priority.
Nf3 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on f3 eyes important squares.
Nbd7 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on d7 eyes important squares.
Re1 places the rook on a central file where it will be most active. Rooks belong on open or semi-open files.
Qa5 brings the queen into the game. Development with purpose — the queen on a5 eyes important squares.
Qc2 brings the queen into the game. Development with purpose — the queen on c2 eyes important squares.
Rfb8 improves the rook's placement. The rook was less effective on f8 and moves to b8 where it serves the plan better.
Rb1 improves the rook's placement. The rook was less effective on a1 and moves to b1 where it serves the plan better.
Nb6 improves the knight's placement. The knight was less effective on d7 and moves to b6 where it serves the plan better.
Nd2 improves the knight's placement. The knight was less effective on f3 and moves to d2 where it serves the plan better.
Nc4 increases Black's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position. We're reaching the critical position of this variation.
Nxc4 captures the knight. This exchange is thematic in the Benko Gambit — it defines the resulting pawn structure and piece activity.
Qxc3 completes the variation. The resulting position after this capture offers Black clear targets and plans. Study this structure — you'll see it often.

The Benko Gambit: Fully Accepted (King Walk) is an important variation of the Benko Gambit. The main line where White accepts both pawns with the king walk maneuver. After Kxf1, g3, and Kg2, White's king is safe on g2. Black plays the standard Benko plan with Bg7, O-O, Nbd7, and rooks to the a and b files. The resulting middlegame is one of the most strategically rich in chess. Let's walk through the key ideas.

The Benko Gambit: Fianchetto System is an important variation of the Benko Gambit. White fianchettos the kingside bishop, creating a solid but passive setup. Black responds with the standard plan of Bg7, O-O, and rooks to the open files. The Nc4 maneuver targets the b2 and a3 weaknesses. After Ra6 and Rba8, the doubled rooks create irresistible pressure. Let's walk through the key ideas.

The Benko Gambit: Declined with Nd2 is an important variation of the Benko Gambit. White declines the full gambit with Nd2, recovering the pawn on c4. Black still gets an active position with d6, Bg7, and Ba6. The Benko spirit lives on even in declined lines — Black's piece placement is natural and the open a-file provides lasting counterplay. Let's walk through the key ideas.

subLines.name: Benko Gambit: Modern with bxa6 g6

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Benko Gambit: Modern with bxa6 g6 is an important variation of the Benko Gambit. A modern move order where Black plays g6 before Bxa6. This can transpose to the main lines but gives Black some flexibility. The key ideas remain the same — fianchetto, trade the light-squared bishop, and apply pressure on the a and b files with rooks. Let's walk through the key ideas.

The Benko Gambit: e6 Break System is an important variation of the Benko Gambit. The e6 break is a powerful pawn lever that opens the position when White has blocked the queenside. After dxe6 fxe6, the e-file opens and Black's rooks become extremely active. Combined with the already-open a and b files, Black has pressure on three files simultaneously. Let's walk through the key ideas.

In the Benko Gambit, the a-File Pressure Trap is a tactical pattern you should know. The doubled rooks on the a-file create tremendous pressure. After Ra7 and Rba8, every piece on a2 and a4 is a target. The knight maneuver to b5 attacks the a3 and c3 squares. White is tied down to defense of the queenside while Black can improve at leisure. Let's walk through how it works.
Nf6 develops the knight to f6. The position looks normal, but the key moment is approaching.
White plays c4, building the position. The trap is already in motion.
c5 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay. The opponent may not realize the trap is being set.
d5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity. The opponent may not realize the trap is being set.
b5 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay. The opponent may not realize the trap is being set.

g3 pushes on the kingside. This pawn advance gains space and can support a future attack toward the enemy king. The opponent may not realize the trap is being set.
Black castles, thinking the position is safe. But danger is lurking.
Kg2 improves the king's placement. The king was less effective on f1 and moves to g2 where it serves the plan better. The opponent may not realize the trap is being set.

Bxf6! Black wins the knight. The trap is complete — the material advantage is decisive. Remember this pattern.

In the Benko Gambit, the Nc6 Fork Trick is a tactical pattern you should know. After White pushes e5 too aggressively, Black can exploit the weakened center. The exchange sacrifice followed by Nc5 and Na4 creates devastating threats. The knight on c3 forks multiple targets and Black's queenside majority becomes a decisive advantage. Let's walk through how it works.
Nf6 develops the knight to f6. The position looks normal, but the key moment is approaching.
White plays c4, building the position. The trap is already in motion.
c5 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay. The opponent may not realize the trap is being set.
